Mini-Case 1.5

OLEH BURDUKOV 12232841

2025-10-06

  • Part a
  • Part b
  • Interpretation of the plot

Part a

finsup_data <- data.frame("Term in years" = c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
                          "Payment" = c(15000, 15500, 16000, 16500, 17000, 17500),
                          "Spot rate" = c(0, 0.0388, 0.0398, 0.0399, 0.0408, 0.0414))
#I create the data frame of parents' payments with the corresponding spot rates
PV_finsup <- sum(finsup_data$Payment*((1 + finsup_data$Spot.rate)^(-finsup_data$Term.in.years)))
#Here I computed the present value of the cash flow stream from my parents

Part b

UK_interestrate_TS <- data.frame( "Maturity in years" = c(1/12, 3/12, 6/12, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 15, 20, 30),
                                  "Present.yield" = c(0.0392, 0.0397, 0.0408, 0.0388, 0.0399, 0.0399, 0.0408, 0.0415, 0.0431, 0.046, 0.0466, 0.0474, 0.0516, 0.0542, 0.0555),
                                  "one week ago" = c(0.0416, 0.0417, 0.041, 0.0395, 0.0404, 0.0404, 0.0412, 0.0419, 0.0434, 0.0462, 0.0468, 0.0476, 0.0519, 0.0546, 0.0557),
                                  "one month ago" = c(0.0417, 0.0417, 0.041, 0.0392, 0.0398, 0.0396, 0.0403, 0.0411, 0.0427, 0.0457, 0.0463, 0.0472, 0.0518, 0.0545, 0.0562)) 
#I compound the data frame with the data from the source with the yield curve
library(tidyr)
UK_IRTS <- UK_interestrate_TS %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c("Present.yield", "one.week.ago", "one.month.ago"),
    names_to = "Term",
    values_to = "Yields")
# This is the data frame with the same data but in long format so the compounding of the plot can be considerably easier
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(UK_IRTS, aes(x = Maturity.in.years, y = Yields, color = Term)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Maturity", y = "Yield",
              title = "UK interest rate term structure")+
  theme_grey()

#At this step I plot the graph of the UK interest rate term structure according to the FT source. The plot can be seen below

Interpretation of the plot

Observing the plot it’s quite easy to see how the future spot rates increase with the length of the period between today and the future term. Also, it’s another useful observation, that today’s yield curve lays slightly below the one from a month ago, if we talk about long term spot rates (15 to 30 years). On the other hand, today’s yield curve displays higher rate of interest for the shorter maturity
